chapter.p: The next morning Henchard went to the Town Hall below LucettaвАЩs house, to attend Petty Sessions, being still a magistrate for the year by virtue of his late position as Mayor. In passing he looked up at her windows, but nothing of her was to be seen.

Henchard as a Justice of the Peace may at first seem to be an even greater incongruity than Shallow and Silence themselves. But his rough and ready perceptions, his sledgehammer directness, had often served him better than nice legal knowledge in despatching such simple business as fell to his hands in this Court. Today Dr. Chalkfield, the Mayor for the year, being absent, the corn-merchant took the big chair, his eyes still abstractedly stretching out of the window to the ashlar front of High-Place Hall.

There was one case only, and the offender stood before him. She was an old woman of mottled countenance, attired in a shawl of that nameless tertiary hue which comes, but cannot be madeвБ†вАФa hue neither tawny, russet, hazel, nor ash; a sticky black bonnet that seemed to have been worn in the country of the Psalmist where the clouds drop fatness; and an apron that had been white in time so comparatively recent as still to contrast visibly with the rest of her clothes. The steeped aspect of the woman as a whole showed her to be no native of the countryside or even of a country-town.

She looked cursorily at Henchard and the second magistrate, and Henchard looked at her, with a momentary pause, as if she had reminded him indistinctly of somebody or something which passed from his mind as quickly as it had come. вАЬWell, and what has she been doing?вАЭ he said, looking down at the charge sheet.
вАЬShe is charged, sir, with the offence of disorderly female and nuisance,вАЭ whispered Stubberd.
вАЬWhere did she do that?вАЭ said the other magistrate.
вАЬBy the church, sir, of all the horrible places in the world!вБ†вАФI caught her in the act, your worship.вАЭ
вАЬStand back then,вАЭ said Henchard, вАЬand letвАЩs hear what youвАЩve got to say.вАЭ
Stubberd was sworn in, the magistrateвАЩs clerk dipped his pen, Henchard being no note-taker himself, and the constable beganвБ†вАФ
вАЬHearing aвАЩ illegal noise I went down the street at twenty-five minutes past eleven p.m. on the night of the fifth instinct, Hannah Dominy. When I hadвБ†вАФвАЭ
вАЬDonвАЩt go so fast, Stubberd,вАЭ said the clerk.

The constable waited, with his eyes on the clerkвАЩs pen, till the latter stopped scratching and said, вАЬyes.вАЭ Stubberd continued: вАЬWhen I had proceeded to the spot I saw defendant at another spot, namely, the gutter.вАЭ He paused, watching the point of the clerkвАЩs pen again.
вАЬGutter, yes, Stubberd.вАЭ

вАЬSpot measuring twelve feet nine inches or thereabouts from where IвБ†вАФвАЭ Still careful not to outrun the clerkвАЩs penmanship Stubberd pulled up again; for having got his evidence by heart it was immaterial to him whereabouts he broke off.
вАЬI object to that,вАЭ spoke up the old woman, вАЬ вАШspot measuring twelve feet nine or thereabouts from where I,вАЩ is not sound testimony!вАЭ
The magistrates consulted, and the second one said that the bench was of opinion that twelve feet nine inches from a man on his oath was admissible.

Stubberd, with a suppressed gaze of victorious rectitude at the old woman, continued: вАЬWas standing myself. She was wambling about quite dangerous to the thoroughfare and when I approached to draw near she committed the nuisance, and insulted me.вАЭ
вАЬ вАШInsulted me.вАЩвБ† вБ†вА¶ Yes, what did she say?вАЭ
вАЬShe said, вАШPut away that dee lantern,вАЩ she says.вАЭ
вАЬYes.вАЭ
вАЬSays she, вАШDost hear, old turmit-head? Put away that dee lantern. I have floored fellows a dee sight finer-looking than a dee fool like thee, you son of a bee, dee me if I haint,вАЩ she says.вАЭ
вАЬI object to that conversation!вАЭ interposed the old woman. вАЬI was not capable enough to hear what I said, and what is said out of my hearing is not evidence.вАЭ

There was another stoppage for consultation, a book was referred to, and finally Stubberd was allowed to go on again. The truth was that the old woman had appeared in court so many more times than the magistrates themselves, that they were obliged to keep a sharp lookout upon their procedure. However, when Stubberd had rambled on a little further Henchard broke out impatiently, вАЬComeвБ†вАФwe donвАЩt want to hear any more of them cust dees and bees! Say the words out like a man, and donвАЩt be so modest, Stubberd; or else leave it alone!вАЭ Turning to the woman, вАЬNow then, have you any questions to ask him, or anything to say?вАЭ
вАЬYes,вАЭ she replied with a twinkle in her eye; and the clerk dipped his pen.
вАЬTwenty years ago or thereabout I was selling of furmity in a tent at Weydon FairвБ†вАФвАЭ
вАЬ вАШTwenty years agoвАЩвБ†вАФwell, thatвАЩs beginning at the beginning; suppose you go back to the Creation!вАЭ said the clerk, not without satire.
But Henchard stared, and quite forgot what was evidence and what was not.

вАЬA man and a woman with a little child came into my tent,вАЭ the woman continued. вАЬThey sat down and had a basin apiece. Ah, LordвАЩs my life! I was of a more respectable station in the world then than I am now, being a land smuggler in a large way of business; and I used to season my furmity with rum for them who asked forвАЩt. I did it for the man; and then he had more and more; till at last he quarrelled with his wife, and offered to sell her to the highest bidder. A sailor came in and bid five guineas, and paid the money, and led her away. And the man who sold his wife in that fashion is the man sitting there in the great big chair.вАЭ The speaker concluded by nodding her head at Henchard and folding her arms.

Everybody looked at Henchard. His face seemed strange, and in tint as if it had been powdered over with ashes. вАЬWe donвАЩt want to hear your life and adventures,вАЭ said the second magistrate sharply, filling the pause which followed. вАЬYouвАЩve been asked if youвАЩve anything to say bearing on the case.вАЭ
вАЬThat bears on the case. It proves that heвАЩs no better than I, and has no right to sit there in judgment upon me.вАЭ
вАЬ вАЩTis a concocted story,вАЭ said the clerk. вАЬSo hold your tongue!вАЭ

вАЬNoвБ†вАФвАЩtis true.вАЭ The words came from Henchard. вАЬ вАЩTis as true as the light,вАЭ he said slowly. вАЬAnd upon my soul it does prove that IвАЩm no better than she! And to keep out of any temptation to treat her hard for her revenge, IвАЩll leave her to you.вАЭ

The sensation in the court was indescribably great. Henchard left the chair, and came out, passing through a group of people on the steps and outside that was much larger than usual; for it seemed that the old furmity dealer had mysteriously hinted to the denizens of the lane in which she had been lodging since her arrival, that she knew a queer thing or two about their great local man Mr. Henchard, if she chose to tell it. This had brought them hither.
вАЬWhy are there so many idlers round the Town Hall today?вАЭ said Lucetta to her servant when the case was over. She had risen late, and had just looked out of the window.
вАЬOh, please, maвАЩam, вАЩtis this larry about Mr. Henchard. A woman has proved that before he became a gentleman he sold his wife for five guineas in a booth at a fair.вАЭ

In all the accounts which Henchard had given her of the separation from his wife Susan for so many years, of his belief in her death, and so on, he had never clearly explained the actual and immediate cause of that separation. The story she now heard for the first time.

A gradual misery overspread LucettaвАЩs face as she dwelt upon the promise wrung from her the night before. At bottom, then, Henchard was this. How terrible a contingency for a woman who should commit herself to his care.

During the day she went out to the Ring, and to other places, not coming in till nearly dusk. As soon as she saw Elizabeth-Jane after her return indoors she told her that she had resolved to go away from home to the seaside for a few daysвБ†вАФto Port-Bredy; Casterbridge was so gloomy.

Elizabeth, seeing that she looked wan and disturbed, encouraged her in the idea, thinking a change would afford her relief. She could not help suspecting that the gloom which seemed to have come over Casterbridge in LucettaвАЩs eyes might be partially owing to the fact that Farfrae was away from home.

Elizabeth saw her friend depart for Port-Bredy, and took charge of High-Place Hall till her return. After two or three days of solitude and incessant rain Henchard called at the house. He seemed disappointed to hear of LucettaвАЩs absence, and though he nodded with outward indifference, he went away handling his beard with a nettled mien.
The next day he called again. вАЬIs she come now?вАЭ he asked.
вАЬYes. She returned this morning,вАЭ replied his stepdaughter. вАЬBut she is not indoors. She has gone for a walk along the turnpike-road to Port-Bredy. She will be home by dusk.вАЭ
After a few words, which only served to reveal his restless impatience, he left the house again.
